---
title: "1.13 Object Creation and Storage"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Constructors create usable objects

Primitive variables store values directly. Object variables store <span class="term">references</span> (引用), and a usable object is created by a <span class="term">constructor</span> (构造器).

```java
World habitat = new World();
Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);
```

The keyword <span class="mark">`new`</span> creates the object and calls the constructor whose parameter list matches the arguments.

## Constructor Syntax

### The constructor name matches the class name

General pattern:

```java
ClassName variableName = new ClassName(arguments);
```

Key rules:

::: {.tight-list}
- The constructor has the <span class="mark">same name as the class</span>.
- The arguments inside `new ClassName(arguments)` must match a constructor signature.
- A constructor initializes the new object's attributes.
:::

## World Constructors

### Default size or chosen size

![World constructor signatures](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-13-object-creation-and-storage/worldConstructors.png){fig-align="center" width="58%"}

```java
World world1 = new World();
World world2 = new World(300, 400);
```

`World()` uses the default world size. `World(int width, int height)` builds a world with chosen dimensions.

## Quick Check

### Which calls match a `World` constructor?

Assume the class provides `World()` and `World(int width, int height)`.

::: {.tight-list}
- `World w = new World();`
- `World w = new World(300, 500);`
- `World w = new World(300);`
- `World w = World(300, 500);`
:::

Correct calls: the first two. The third does not match the parameter list, and the fourth is missing <span class="mark">`new`</span>.

## Overloaded Constructors

### Same name, different parameter lists

Constructors are <span class="term">overloaded</span> when a class defines more than one constructor with the same name but different parameter lists.

```java
World()
World(int width, int height)
```

When Java sees `new World(300, 400)`, it chooses the constructor that expects two `int` arguments.

## Turtle Constructors

### Required arguments and argument order

```java
Turtle t1 = new Turtle(world1);
Turtle t2 = new Turtle(50, 100, world1);
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `Turtle` always needs a `World` so it has somewhere to draw.
- Some constructors use only the world.
- Some constructors use <span class="mark">`x, y, world`</span> in that exact order.
- Wrong argument order gives a type mismatch or an incorrect result.
:::

![Turtle constructor signatures](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-13-object-creation-and-storage/TurtleClassDefn.png){fig-align="center" width="66%"}

## Code Task {.fit-small}

### Constructor experiment

Start with this working program.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtleConstructorTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        World world = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle t1 = new Turtle(world);
        Turtle t2 = new Turtle(50, 100, world);
        world.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Code Task

### Constructor experiment edits

Use the starter code from the previous slide.

Classroom edits:

::: {.tight-list}
- Change the world to `300` by `400`.
- Move `t2` to the top-right area by changing the `x` and `y` arguments.
- Predict what happens if the arguments are placed in the wrong order.
:::

## Object Variables and References

### A variable can refer to an object or to `null`

```java
World world1 = new World();
Turtle t1 = null;
t1 = new Turtle(world1);
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A reference variable does <span class="mark">not</span> contain the whole object.
- It stores a way to find that object.
- `null` means the variable does not refer to any object yet.
- Calling a method on `null` causes a runtime error.
:::

## Reading Constructor Signatures {.image-fit}

### Parameters tell you what the constructor expects

![Date constructors](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-13-object-creation-and-storage/DateClass.png){fig-align="center" width="66%"}

The <span class="term">signature</span> shows the constructor name and parameter list.

## Reading Constructor Signatures {.image-fit}

### Map arguments to parameters

![Parameter mapping](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-13-object-creation-and-storage/parameterMappingDate.png){fig-align="center" width="64%"}

The argument values in the call are copied into the matching parameter variables.

## Date Constructor Call

### Match arguments to parameters

```java
Date birthday = new Date(2020, 9, 20);
```

In this call:

::: {.tight-list}
- `2020`, `9`, and `20` are <span class="term">arguments</span> (实参).
- `year`, `month`, and `day` are <span class="term">parameters</span> (形参) in the constructor.
- Types and order must match the constructor signature.
:::

## Quick Check

### Parameter or argument?

Constructor header:

```java
public World(int width, int height)
```

Constructor call:

```java
World habitat = new World(150, 200);
```

Answer:

::: {.tight-list}
- `width` and `height` are parameters.
- `150` and `200` are arguments.
:::

## Call by Value

### Arguments are copied into parameters

Java uses <span class="term">call by value</span> when passing information into constructors and methods.

```java
int w = 150;
int h = 200;
World habitat = new World(w, h);
```

The constructor receives copies of the values in `w` and `h`. After the constructor finishes, control returns to the next statement after the call.

## Debugging Task {.fit-small}

### Fix the constructor and reference errors

```java
public class TurtleConstructorDebug {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        World w = new World(300, 0);
        turtle t0;
        Turtle t1 = new Turtle();
        Turtle t2 = new Turtle(world, 100, 50)
        t0.forward();
        t1.turnRight();
        t2.turnLeft();
        world.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Debugging Task

### Fix categories

Use the broken code from the previous slide.

Fix categories:

::: {.tight-list}
- invalid world size
- class-name capitalization
- missing world reference
- wrong constructor argument order
- missing semicolon
- calling a method before a variable refers to an object
:::

## Debugging Task

### One possible corrected version

```java
public class TurtleConstructorDebug {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        World world = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle t0 = new Turtle(world);
        Turtle t1 = new Turtle(world);
        Turtle t2 = new Turtle(100, 50, world);

        t0.forward();
        t1.turnRight();
        t2.turnLeft();
        world.show(true);
    }
}
```

Students should be able to explain which constructor each line calls.

## Coding Challenge

### Custom Turtles

![Custom turtles challenge](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-13-object-creation-and-storage/customTurtles.png){fig-align="center" width="42%"}

Create several `CustomTurtle` objects with different position, body color, shell color, width, and height.

```java
CustomTurtle turtle1 =
    new CustomTurtle(150, 300, world1, Color.green, Color.blue, 150, 200);

CustomTurtle turtle2 =
    new CustomTurtle(350, 200, world1, Color.red, Color.yellow, 25, 50);
```

## Coding Challenge

### Constructor design requirements

Complete the classroom task:

::: {.tight-list}
- Add the two required `CustomTurtle` objects.
- Create one additional turtle design of your own.
- Use at least two different colors.
- Explain which argument controls location, color, width, and height.
:::

This task checks whether the constructor call matches the class documentation.

## AP-Style Check {.fit-small}

### Constructor signatures

Suppose `Cat` has these constructors:

```java
Cat()
Cat(String name)
Cat(String name, int age)
```

Which statements are valid?

::: {.tight-list}
- `Cat c1 = new Cat();`
- `Cat c2 = new Cat("Milo");`
- `Cat c3 = new Cat("Milo", 4);`
- `Cat c4 = new Cat(4, "Milo");`
:::

The first three are valid. The last one has the wrong argument order and types.

## AP-Style Check

### References and `null`

```java
Turtle t = null;
World w = new World();
t = new Turtle(w);
```

After these statements:

::: {.tight-list}
- `w` refers to a `World` object.
- `t` refers to a `Turtle` object.
- `t` is no longer `null`.
:::

The object variable stores a reference, not the complete object data.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that constructors create and initialize objects
- identify constructor syntax with <span class="mark">`new ClassName(arguments)`</span>
- distinguish overloaded constructors by their parameter lists
- explain that object variables store references
- recognize `null` as no object reference
- distinguish parameters from arguments
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
